text: Easy to change voice style & speed with Grok
embedded_text:
Grok Voice Mode lets you pick from a variety of voices:

• Ara - Upbeat Female
• Eve - Soothing Female
• Leo - British Male
• Rex - Calm Male
• Sal - Smooth Male
• Gork - Lazy Male

Just tap the settings icon and choose your voice.

text: So many countries have a collapsing birth rate
embedded_text:
BREAKING: Europe is facing a historic birth collapse.

• Finland down a third since 2010.
• Switzerland, England, Scotland, Wales at record lows.
• Poland lowest in 200 years.
• Greece lowest in Europe.
• Spain, Italy, Austria, Germany in over 35 years of very low fertility. https://t.co/0ReqyO6w8G

text:
There are very few people on Earth. 

Living in a big city gives you the illusion that Earth is full, but most of Earth is empty of people.
embedded_text:
Elon Musk
 "All 8 billion people on Earth can fit on one floor in the city of New York. Another way, if you're flying across the country and your goal is to drop a water balloon on someone, you will fail because it's empty." https://t.co/3g88jMLzcC

text:
Looks like we can bring power consumption down closer to 250 Watts, which is a big deal for Optimus.

Important to note that AI5 is a specialized inference chip for the Tesla AI software. That said, it will perform – for our purposes – much better than anything else available. 

To borrow Jensen’s phrase, we wouldn’t use any other chip in our cars and robots even if they were free!

760,000 people per seat.
40,000,000 illegals.

That’s what this is all about.

Power.

Every 760,000 illegals deported the Democrats lose a seat.

Below are the other major RLTs available today, plus the most advanced pipeline candidates.\\nLutathera\\u00ae (lutetium Lu 177 dotatate) \\u2013 Novartis\\nTarget: Somatostatin receptors (SSTR) on neuroendocrine tumors (NETs)\\nApproved: \\n  - Adults (2018) with progressive, SSTR-positive gastroenteropancreatic NETs (GEP-NETs)\\n  - Pediatric patients \\u226512 years (April 2024) \\u2013 first RLT ever approved for children\\nRegimen: 4 infusions of 7.4 GBq every 8 weeks + long-acting octreotide\\nKey data: NETTER-1 trial \\u2192 79% lower risk of progression\\/death vs high-dose octreotide; NETTER-2 (1st-line) \\u2192 72% reduction in progression risk\\nSide effects: Nausea, fatigue, low blood counts, kidney protection mandatory\\nXofigo\\u00ae (radium-223 dichloride) \\u2013 Bayer\\nTarget: Bone metastases (mimics calcium, incorporates into bone matrix)\\nApproved: 2013 for castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) with symptomatic bone mets and no visceral mets\\nRegimen: 6 monthly IV injections\\nKey data: ALSYMPCA trial \\u2192 3.6-month overall survival benefit; pain palliation\\nNote: Technically a targeted alpha therapy, but grouped with RLTs in most centers\\nZevalin\\u00ae (ibritumomab tiuxetan) \\u2013 Spectrum\\/Acrotech\\nTarget: CD20 on B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL)\\nApproved: 2002 (oldest modern RLT)\\nRegimen: Single therapeutic dose of yttrium-90 Zevalin after rituximab priming + imaging dose\\nUse: Relapsed\\/refractory low-grade or follicular NHL; consolidation after remission\\nPipeline RLTs closest to approval (Phase 3)\\n| Candidate | Sponsor | Target | Radioisotope | Expected |\\n|-----------|---------|--------|--------------|----------|\\n| 177Lu-edotreotide | RadioMedix\\/ITM | SSTR (NETs) | Lutetium-177 | Direct Lutathera competitor; COMPETE trial completed |\\n| 225Ac-PSMA | Multiple academic sites | PSMA (prostate) | Actinium-225 (alpha) | \\u201cLast-line\\u201d salvage; dramatic PSA drops in compassionate-use studies |\\n| AAA817 | Novartis | PSMA (prostate) | Actinium-225 | Next-gen Pluvicto; two Phase 3 trials launched 2025 |\\nEmerging targets in earlier trials\\nFAP (fibroblast activation protein) \\u2192 stromal targeting in many solid tumors\\nCAIX (carbonic anhydrase IX) \\u2192 clear-cell kidney cancer\\nHER2 \\u2192 breast & gastric cancers\\nDLL3 \\u2192 small-cell lung cancer (Lilly\\/BMS programs)\\nQuick comparison table\\n| Therapy | Cancer | Requires pre-scan? | Cycles | Main toxicity |\\n|---------|--------|---------------------|--------|---------------|\\n| Pluvicto | PSMA+ mCRPC | PSMA-PET | 4\\u20136 q6w | Dry mouth, nausea, cytopenias |\\n| Lutathera | SSTR+ NET | OctreoScan \\/ 68Ga-DOTATATE | 4 q8w | Kidney, marrow |\\n| Xofigo | Bone-only CRPC | Bone scan | 6 q4w | Marrow, fractures |\\n| Zevalin | CD20+ NHL | 111In imaging dose | 1 | Prolonged cytopenias |\\nBottom line\\nIf Scott Adams\\u2019 cancer ever progresses beyond PSMA-targeted therapy, Lutathera (if SSTR-positive) or Xofigo (if bone-dominant) are the only other FDA-approved RLTs today. {"items": [{"media_urls": [], "message": "Other radioligand therapies"}, {"deepsearch_headers": [{"steps": [{}, {}]}], "media_urls": [], "message": "Pluvicto (lutetium Lu 177 vipivotide tetraxetan) is one of several FDA-approved **radioligand therapies (RLTs)**—precision drugs that pair a targeting molecule (ligand) with a radioactive payload to destroy cancer cells while sparing healthy tissue. Below are the other major RLTs available today, plus the most advanced pipeline candidates.\n\n### 1. Lutathera® (lutetium Lu 177 dotatate) – Novartis\n- **Target**: Somatostatin receptors (SSTR) on neuroendocrine tumors (NETs)\n- **Approved**: \n  - Adults (2018) with progressive, SSTR-positive gastroenteropancreatic NETs (GEP-NETs)\n  - Pediatric patients ≥12 years (April 2024) – first RLT ever approved for children\n- **Regimen**: 4 infusions of 7.4 GBq every 8 weeks + long-acting octreotide\n- **Key data**: NETTER-1 trial → 79% lower risk of progression/death vs high-dose octreotide; NETTER-2 (1st-line) → 72% reduction in progression risk\n- **Side effects**: Nausea, fatigue, low blood counts, kidney protection mandatory\n\n### 2. Xofigo® (radium-223 dichloride) – Bayer\n- **Target**: Bone metastases (mimics calcium, incorporates into bone matrix)\n- **Approved**: 2013 for castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) with symptomatic bone mets and **no** visceral mets\n- **Regimen**: 6 monthly IV injections\n- **Key data**: ALSYMPCA trial → 3.6-month overall survival benefit; pain palliation\n- **Note**: Technically a targeted alpha therapy, but grouped with RLTs in most centers\n\n### 3. Zevalin® (ibritumomab tiuxetan) – Spectrum/Acrotech\n- **Target**: CD20 on B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL)\n- **Approved**: 2002 (oldest modern RLT)\n- **Regimen**: Single therapeutic dose of yttrium-90 Zevalin after rituximab priming + imaging dose\n- **Use**: Relapsed/refractory low-grade or follicular NHL; consolidation after remission\n\n### Pipeline RLTs closest to approval (Phase 3)\n| Candidate | Sponsor | Target | Radioisotope | Expected |\n|-----------|---------|--------|--------------|----------|\n| 177Lu-edotreotide | RadioMedix/ITM | SSTR (NETs) | Lutetium-177 | Direct Lutathera competitor; COMPETE trial completed |\n| 225Ac-PSMA | Multiple academic sites | PSMA (prostate) | Actinium-225 (alpha) | “Last-line” salvage; dramatic PSA drops in compassionate-use studies |\n| AAA817 | Novartis | PSMA (prostate) | Actinium-225 | Next-gen Pluvicto; two Phase 3 trials launched 2025 |\n\n### Emerging targets in earlier trials\n- **FAP** (fibroblast activation protein) → stromal targeting in many solid tumors\n- **CAIX** (carbonic anhydrase IX) → clear-cell kidney cancer\n- **HER2** → breast & gastric cancers\n- **DLL3** → small-cell lung cancer (Lilly/BMS programs)\n\n### Quick comparison table\n\n| Therapy | Cancer | Requires pre-scan? | Cycles | Main toxicity |\n|---------|--------|---------------------|--------|---------------|\n| Pluvicto | PSMA+ mCRPC | PSMA-PET | 4–6 q6w | Dry mouth, nausea, cytopenias |\n| Lutathera | SSTR+ NET | OctreoScan / 68Ga-DOTATATE | 4 q8w | Kidney, marrow |\n| Xofigo | Bone-only CRPC | Bone scan | 6 q4w | Marrow, fractures |\n| Zevalin | CD20+ NHL | 111In imaging dose | 1 | Prolonged cytopenias |\n\n### Bottom line\nIf Scott Adams’ cancer ever progresses beyond PSMA-targeted therapy, **Lutathera** (if SSTR-positive) or **Xofigo** (if bone-dominant) are the only other FDA-approved RLTs today. Clinical-trial options using alpha-emitters or new ligands are opening weekly—ask his oncologist about trials on ClinicalTrials.gov (search “radioligand prostate” or “actinium PSMA”).\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n"}]}, "is_translatable": false, "legacy": {"bookmark_count": 483, "bookmarked": false, "conversation_id_str": "1985407720128987487", "created_at": "Mon Nov 03 18:56:37 +0000 2025", "display_text_range": [16, 92], "entities": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "timestamps": [], "urls": [{"display_url": "x.com/i/grok/share/U…", "expanded_url": "https://x.com/i/grok/share/U3YAtMbJP3mjzD3c9iUaSOEy2", "indices": [69, 92], "url": "https://t.co/rLYK7sdUsI"}], "user_mentions": [{"id_str": "2853461537", "indices": [0, 15], "name": "Scott Adams", "screen_name": "ScottAdamsSays"}]}, "favorite_count": 6910, "favorited": false, "full_text": "@ScottAdamsSays Some potential alternatives if Pluvicto doesn’t work https://t.co/rLYK7sdUsI", "id_str": "1985420868626104427", "in_reply_to_screen_name": "ScottAdamsSays", "in_reply_to_status_id_str": "1985407720128987487", "in_reply_to_user_id_str": "2853461537", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "possibly_sensitive": false, "possibly_sensitive_editable": true, "quote_count": 18, "reply_count": 194, "retweet_count": 443, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "44196397"}, "rest_id": "1985420868626104427", "source": "<a href=\"http://twitter.com/download/iphone\" rel=\"nofollow\">Twitter for iPhone</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "202973", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
President @realDonaldTrump is right. 

We must distinguish clearly between companies that need to hire critical world-class talent from other countries vs companies that simply hire lower cost non-US employees to increase profits. 

SpaceX has succeeded *despite* not being unable to hire critical world-class talent from other countries due to ITAR laws. Had we been able to do so, our progress would have been faster. 

Tesla, which is not restricted by ITAR, progress has been greatly enhanced due to being able to hire world-class people from other countries, in addition to hiring every ace we can find in America.

text:
By next month, Grok will literally look at and understand all ~100 million 𝕏 posts per day (including images and video), no matter how small the account, and recommend content to users based on the intrinsic quality of the content itself.

This is only possible with advanced AI using a lot of GPUs and will massively improve the quality of everyone’s feed.

Move to Austin! It gets better very year. 

So many cool, world-class people are moving to Austin from SF, LA, NY, London and many other parts of the world!

Austin++

After living in Austin metro for the last 5 years, I can attest that this area of the country is AWESOME.

But two HUGE drawbacks:

The growth is CRAZY, which means that regions will get disrupted very quickly. Where you end up living might look completely different in 3 years.

text:
Nikita deserves a lot of credit for fixing many things about 𝕏, but is not working on the algorithm. Blame for me problems with the algorithm – it still has a long way to go to be great. 

Transitioning from an inscrutable hairball of heuristics, which was the old Twitter algorithm, to a pure Grok-based AI recommendation system is being done by a team mostly from the @xAI side that reports directly to me. Same with making advertising relevant to users, which basically didn’t exist in legacy Twitter.

The engineering teams building 𝕏 Chat, which is encrypted and has audio/video calling and file transfer, and 𝕏 Money (launching to the public soon) also report to me, although I’ve recently asked Nikita to get involved as he finishes up other work. 

If anyone out there wants to help make these products insanely awesome, please join us!

Basically... I'm amazed - it drives really, really well, smooth, confident, noticeably better than what I'm used to on HW3 (my previous car) and eons ahead of the version I remember driving up highway 280 on my first day at Tesla ~9 years ago, where I had to intervene every time the road mildly curved or sloped. (note this is v13, my car hasn't been offered the latest v14 yet) On the highway, I felt like a passenger in some super high tech Maglev train pod - the car is locked in the center of the lane while I'm looking out from Model X's higher vantage point and its panoramic front window, listening to the (incredible) sound system, or chatting with Grok. On city streets, the car casually handled a number of tricky scenarios that I remember losing sleep over just a few years ago. It negotiated incoming cars in tight lanes, it gracefully went around construction and temporarily in-lane stationary cars, it correctly timed tricky left turns with incoming traffic from both sides, it gracefully gave way to the car that went out of order in the 4-way stop sign, it found a way to squeeze into a bumper to bumper traffic to make its turn, it overtook the bus that was loading passengers but still stopped for the stop sign that was blocked by the bus, and at the end of the route it circled around a parking lot, found a spot and... parked. Basically a flawless drive. For context, I'm used to going out for a brief test drive around the neighborhood to return with 20 clips of things that could be improved. It's new for me to do just that and exactly like I used to, but come back with nothing. Perfect drive, no notes. I expect there's still more work for the team in the long march of 9s, but it's just so cool to see that we're beyond finding issues on any individual ~1 hour drive around the neighborhood, you actually have to go to the fleet and mine them. Back then, I processed the incredible promise of vehicle autonomy at scale (in the fully scaleable, vision only, end-to-end Tesla way) only intellectually, but now it is possible to feel it intuitively too if you just go out for a drive. Wait, of course surround video stream at 60Hz processed by a fully dedicated "driving brain" neural net will work, and it will be so much better and safer than a human driver. Did anyone else think otherwise? I also watched @aelluswamy 's new ICCV25 talk last week (https://t.co/RdaM23kvez) that hints at some of the recent under the hood technical components driving this progress. Sensor streams (videos, maps, kinematics, audio, ...) over long contexts (e.g. ~30 seconds) go into a big neural net, steering/acceleration comes out, optionally with visualization auxiliary data. This is the dream of the complete Software 1.0 -> Software 2.0 re-write that scales fully with data streaming from millions of cars in the fleet and the compute capacity of your chip, not some engineer's clever new DoubleParkedCarHandler C++ abstraction with undefined test-time characteristics of memory and runtime. There's a lot more hints in the video on where things are going with the emerging "robotics+AI at scale stack". World reconstructors, world simulators "dreaming" dynamics, RL, all of these components general, foundational, neural net based, how the car is really just one kind of robot... are people getting this yet? Huge congrats to the team - you're building magic objects of the future, you rock! And I love my car <3."}}}, "rest_id": "1988705360723763242", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "17936729", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "1988712639598653528", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "12972167", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: Woodrow Wilson should be very low in presidential rankings. He caused a lot of harm to America and the world.

Delaware became the dominant state for incorporation because New Jersey - under Woodrow Wilson - put the screws to business. Delaware said, ‘we will provide a stable environment with clear law.’

Delaware is now throwing away the only real reason for the state to exist at all.

{"items": [{"media_urls": [], "message": "What if you dig a hole?"}, {"deepsearch_headers": [{"steps": [{}]}], "media_urls": [], "message": "Digging a hole on the Moon changes everything — and turns Elon’s “shadowed crater” idea into a **winner**. Here’s the cheat-sheet: 1. **Cosmic-ray shielding** - 1 meter of lunar regolith = 100 g/cm² → blocks **all** galactic cosmic rays. - Earth’s best underground lab (SNOLAB) sits under 6000 g/cm² of rock. Overkill. → **Moon wins** with 1/100th the digging. 2. **Temperature** - Dig 1.5 m under the floor of a permanently shadowed crater. - You hit **20–30 K** year-round, no cryo plant needed. - Earth’s coldest dilution fridges still burn 1 kW per mK of cooling power. → **Moon wins** on electricity bill. 3. **Vacuum** - Native 10⁻¹² torr. - No outgassing from air, no pumps, no leaks. → **Moon wins** again. 4. **Power & logistics** - 50 kW solar array on the crater rim + 1 km fiber-optic cable → 24/7 power. - Starship lands 100 m away once per year with new chips. → **Earth loses** on transport cost. 5. **Radiation-hard qubits** - Google/McEwen 2024 paper: surface-code overhead drops 40× under 1 m regolith. - 1000 logical qubits → 1 million physical qubits → fits in a 3 m × 3 m × 3 m hole. One hole. One Starship. One year. You now have the **quietest, coldest, cheapest** quantum computer in the solar system. Elon was right — he just forgot to bring a shovel. Who’s got a spare Starship and a backhoe?"}]}, "is_translatable": false, "legacy": {"bookmark_count": 78, "bookmarked": false, "conversation_id_str": "1985416165372342702", "created_at": "Wed Nov 05 19:21:17 +0000 2025", "display_text_range": [14, 78], "entities": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "timestamps": [], "urls": [{"display_url": "x.com/i/grok/share/i…", "expanded_url": "https://x.com/i/grok/share/i1HrhGI2iTKDAArSRPo17xQP1", "indices": [55, 78], "url": "https://t.co/AtNDdIZWQ5"}], "user_mentions": [{"id_str": "1415722542422183937", "indices": [0, 13], "name": "Martin Bauer", "screen_name": "martinmbauer"}]}, "favorite_count": 1173, "favorited": false, "full_text": "@martinmbauer Just dig a hole, you clueless monkey 🐒 https://t.co/AtNDdIZWQ5", "id_str": "1986151854489927694", "in_reply_to_screen_name": "elonmusk", "in_reply_to_status_id_str": "1985744333845275108", "in_reply_to_user_id_str": "44196397", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "possibly_sensitive": false, "possibly_sensitive_editable": true, "quote_count": 35, "reply_count": 266, "retweet_count": 74, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "44196397"}, "rest_id": "1986151854489927694", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "130337", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

embedded_text:

You can now edit any prompt easily, just tap the “Edit button” and customize it your way.

Plus, you’ll get a preview of all video versions you’ve created, so you can pick your favorite before saving.

Update your Grok app to https://t.co/e1aUcYbVtv

In data centers each chip is bathed in jacuzzi and babysat by nannies. If they died it would be hot swapped by one of their clones. The fault rate of GPUs in datacenter is actually quite high. Industrial average annual fault rate of H100 is 9%. Ideal conditions could reduce it down to 2% but never below a single digit. The fault recovery of GPU nodes actually could take a while, from minutes to hours. It is not instantaneous. In robots, the chips are out in the cold and they need rapid self recovery. The fault tolerance is in a different league. It is not uncommon many robotic companies struggle to get the chip running more than a few hours without rebooting. For chip companies, this is great since they would tell robotic companies to buy more chips for hot swapping. For robotic companies, this is bad since it is obviously not a scaleable solution but they are stuck with endless back-and-forth JIRA tickets with vendors."}}}, "rest_id": "1987200430938456068", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "17292637", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "1987273588257529914", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "16702643", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Slightly different versions of the Tesla AI5 chip will be made at TSMC and Samsung simply because they translate designs to physical form differently, but the goal is that our AI software works identically.

We will have samples and maybe a small number of units in 2026, but high volume production is only possible in 2027. 

AI6 will use the same fabs, but achieve roughly 2X performance. Aiming for a fast follow to AI5, so hopefully mid 2028 for volume production of AI6.

AI7 will need different fabs, as it is more adventurous.

text: You can now upscale videos with one tap on Grok Imagine!
embedded_text:
While making a video with Grok Imagine, use the Upscale feature to turn your video into an HDR version. 

Just select Upscale from the options drop-down menu. https://t.co/4CYH0IvNuj

The rest is noise. “The sun is about 99.8% of the mass of the solar system. Jupiter's about 0.1% and everything else is in the remaining 0.1%, and we are much less than 0.1%. So, if you burnt all of the mass of the solar system, then the total energy produced by the sun would still round up to 100%. If you just burnt Earth, the whole planet, and burnt Jupiter, which is very big and quite challenging to burn, turn Jupiter into a thermonuclear reactor, the sun is 99.8% of the mass of the solar system and everything else is in the miscellaneous category. Basically, no matter what you do, total energy produced in our solar system rounds up to 100% from the sun. You could even throw another Jupiter in, so we're going to snag a Jupiter from somewhere else, you could teleport two more Jupiters into our solar system, burn them, and the sun would still round up to 100%. As long as you're at 99.6%, you're still rounding up to 100%. Maybe that gives some perspective of why solar is really the thing that matters. And, as soon as you start thinking about things at a grander scale, like Kardashev Scale 2 civilizations, it becomes very, very obvious. I'm not saying anything that's new, by the way. Anyone who studies physics has known this for a very long time. In fact, Kardashev, a Russian physicist who came up with this idea, I think, in the 1960s, just as a way to classify civilizations, where Kardashev Scale 1 would be, you've harnessed most of the energy of the planet, Kardashev Scale 2, you've harnessed most of the energy of your sun, Kardashev 3, you've harnessed most of the energy of a galaxy. Now we're only about 1% or a few percent of Kardashev Scale 1 right now, optimistically. But as soon as you go to Kardashev Scale 2, where you're talking about the power of the sun, then you're really just saying everything is solar power and the rest is in the noise. text:
Not sure if it’s super new, as we’ve been doing something along these lines at Tesla for a while, but it does make sense. 

The single biggest technical challenge of Tesla self-driving AI is context compression of 1.5GB/s of video to ~2kB/s of control outputs using a puny inference computer without making any mistakes. 

Super hard to avoid overly lossy compression at any given step.

8810, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 8910}]}, "text": "Baron Capital Supports Elon Musk’s 2025 CEO Performance Award Baron Capital is in support of the proposed 2025 CEO Performance Award. We believe it reflects the same principles of accountability, performance and alignment with shareholder interests that drove Tesla’s past success. The plan ensures that shareholders win first, and that Elon Musk continues to lead Tesla for many years to come. We commend the Board for recognizing this and for working to retain Tesla’s most valuable asset. We believe the 2025 CEO Performance Award is right for the company, right for shareholders and right for the future. Baron Capital has been investing in @Tesla since 2014. Our shareholders and clients have benefited enormously from @elonmusk’s vision, determination and execution. Tesla’s success and its future are inseparable from Elon. Elon is the ultimate “key man” of key man risk. Without his relentless drive and uncompromising standards, there would be no Tesla. He has built one of the most important companies in the world. He’s redefining transportation, energy and humanoid robotics and creating lasting value for shareholders while doing it. His interests are completely aligned with investors. He owns 13% of the company. We will be voting FOR. Supplemental Information When my analyst team met with Elon Musk for two hours in our New York City office in 2010 during Tesla’s IPO roadshow, we thought his plans were more hopeful than plausible. Only one of our Funds made a small investment in Tesla at that time. But because Elon is a very impressive executive, we monitored Tesla closely for the next few years. Our research included several visits to Tesla's factories in Fremont, California and Reno, Nevada and frequent phone calls with Tesla executives including Elon. In 2014 when sales of Model S began to increase significantly, I concluded we had been wrong thinking that Elon would not succeed. From 2014 to 2016 Baron Capital purchased approximately $400 million Tesla shares at prices that were about 13X higher than Tesla's IPO price! Tesla's shares have since increased more than 30X our purchase price in 2014. Baron Capital has earned about $8 billion profits realized and unrealized from our Tesla shares for our clients and proprietary accounts. We began to purchase shares in another Musk business, @SpaceX, in 2017. We have invested about $1.3 billion to date in SpaceX. We have since earned about $4 billion profits realized and unrealized on those SpaceX purchases. Our recent $338 million investment in Elon's @xAI data center, social network and Grok AI, has produced about $340 million profits realized and unrealized to date. Profits earned from Musk-controlled businesses represent about 24% of the $52 billion profits we have earned for clients and proprietary accounts since 1992 when we managed $100 million in assets. Musk entities, due to their significant capital appreciation, now represent about 26% of our Firm's $44 billion AUM. We believe that Baron Capital could earn at least 5X the profits we have generated to date from the Musk ecosystem by 2035. If we are right, the beneficiaries of this success will be the estimated 1 million-plus working class and mass affluent, Baron mutual fund shareholders...their IRAs and 401(k) retirement accounts...as well as our institutional clients including endowments, foundations and sovereigns...and, looking through those entities, millions of additional individuals. Further, we think the impact of our investments in Tesla is far greater than the amounts we manage directly. This belief is based on the fact that virtually every day when I am walking on the streets of New York City or in restaurants or traveling...people approach me to say, "Thank you, Ron. You have changed my life!" That is since, for the past twelve years, I have appeared on CNBC’s Squawk Box three or four times a year and have spoken about Tesla...and lately SpaceX, too...and investors have obviously been watching and listening. Tesla's bonus contract with Elon has been written to achieve an objective to get Robotaxi and Optimus robots to scale. If successful, Tesla could earn $400 billion per year in adjusted EBITDA for an extended period by 2035. That could produce a market capitalization for Tesla of over $8 trillion. We believe by 2045 Tesla value could quadruple its 2035 levels. Tesla’s current market cap is $1.5 trillion. Elon will only achieve his full bonus payout if those objectives are met. Tesla shareholders benefit before he does by reaching aggressive market cap objectives that few believe can be attained. We think it is unlikely at this time anyone, but Elon could accomplish those objectives. We believe Elon is not only perhaps the most extraordinary engineer on our planet but an unusually competent businessperson. If he did not live in this country, we think it improbable there would be electric cars...reusable space rockets...Starlink broadband satellites...self-driving cars...giant storage batteries that can be added to America's electric utility grid quickly to provide required energy for AI data centers’ growth in our country...a satellite system protecting our homeland...super-fast manufacturing lines...AI digitizing physical processes...and who knows what else... We also deem it important that another element in Elon's bonus contract is succession. Tesla has 124,000 employees. The company hires only a small fraction of the millions of applicants from top engineering schools for Tesla engineering jobs. I found the comment of one Tesla Board member to me recently especially memorable: "Other AI businesses hire mercenary engineers. Tesla hires brilliant young engineers and trains them. Why would they ever want to leave?" Elon meets regularly with 30 to 40 engineers at a time in a room to discuss their work in various disciplines. And help solve problems they may be facing. Another Board member noted that when those employees leave the room, most are shaking their heads. The common refrain is that solutions to tasks they had thought unsolvable were outlined by Elon. On the fly. "I can't believe what we are learning," was heard most often from the best and brightest. It is rare that a senior executive challenges his prior assumptions recursively. Elon does. In my opinion, culture and people at Musk companies are unmatched at any publicly owned business I have visited in my 55-year career. That's because of him. From the large pool of awesome, talented engineers Elon is assembling, it should be possible to create an executive team to succeed Elon at some distant future point...if he ever decides he's done... If Elon ever leaves Tesla, we believe it's certainly not to sit on a beach somewhere drinking Mai Tai cocktails! On Mars, maybe. But a beach in the Caribbean? No way. No more scattered menus. No more hunting for the “encrypted” tab. Everything lives in one clean feed - exactly how Elon’s been teasing the all-in-one X app to work. Encrypted chats now support E2E messages, media, files, and even audio/video calls - Elon calls it “Bitcoin-style encryption,” built for real privacy without breaking functionality. Old messages simply get labeled as unencrypted. Turn on encryption, set your PIN, and you’re in. Simple, not cryptic. It’s a quiet but major step toward X becoming a universal communications hub - part messenger, part phone, part file-share, part everything. What comes next? Maybe smoother cross-device syncing, more super-app features, and tighter integration as XChat becomes the backbone of the platform. And expect critics to complain while users quietly adopt it - because convenience always wins. X just made messaging feel modern again. The new name comes from Isaac Asimov’s Foundation, where the “Encyclopedia Galactica” is like the entire internet and your smartest teachers rolled into one, but for an entire galaxy. Elon wants Grokipedia to be that, but real. With audio, images, videos, and maybe even fewer weird donation banners. Launched in 2025, Grokipedia started with over 885,000 articles and a mission: delete the drama. This isn’t just about facts, not narratives. It's Elon’s way of saying: if humanity’s going down, at least we’ll go down informed. 1. for longer movies , we need an option to organize like a project style, and to be able to add main prompts like the niche of the current movie, the character description and to be able to select a custom seed so we can have consistency of the characters.

dilution refrigerators","sender":"USER","grokMode":"NORMAL","mediaUrls":[]},{"message":"What Is a Dilution Refrigerator?\nA dilution refrigerator is a cryogenic device that achieves continuous cooling to temperatures below 1 K (as low as ~2\u201310 mK, or 0.002\u20130.010 K) by exploiting the quantum phase-separation of liquid mixtures of the two helium isotopes: \u00b3He (helium-3) and \u2074He (helium-4).\nIt is the standard tool for ultra-low-temperature physics, quantum computing, astronomy detectors, and materials research requiring milli-Kelvin environments.\n--\nKey Principle: \u00b3He\u2013\u2074He Phase Separation\nBelow ~0.87 K, a mixture of \u00b3He and \u2074He spontaneously separates into two immiscible liquid phases:\n - Concentrated phase: almost pure \u00b3He (light, floats on top).\n - Dilute phase: mostly \u2074He with ~6.6% \u00b3He dissolved in it (heavier, at the bottom).\n\u00b3He atoms prefer the concentrated phase, but some dissolve into the dilute phase due to entropy of mixing.\nFermi statistics (\u00b3He is a fermion) make the dilute phase behave like a Fermi liquid with very low heat capacity.\n--\nHow Cooling Works (The Dilution Cycle)\nThe core is a mixing chamber containing the two phases in contact.\nEvaporation-like process:\n - \u00b3He atoms cross from the concentrated phase into the dilute phase.\n - This absorbs heat because it increases entropy (mixing).\n\u00b3He is removed from the dilute phase by pumping it away through a still (heated to ~0.7 K).\n - In the still, \u00b3He evaporates more easily than \u2074He (due to zero-point energy differences).\n - Pumping lowers the \u00b3He concentration in the dilute phase \u2192 more \u00b3He flows in from the concentrated phase \u2192 continuous cooling.\nCirculation:\n - Pumped \u00b3He gas is cooled, condensed, and returned to the concentrated phase.\n - This forms a closed refrigeration cycle (no net helium loss).\n--\nSchematic (Text-Based)\n`\n[External Pumps] \u2190 \u00b3He gas (warm)\n \u2193\n[Condenser] \u2192 liquid \u00b3He \u2192 [Concentrated phase]\n \u2193 \u2191\n ---------------------\u2192 [Phase boundary]\n \u2193\n [Dilute phase] \u2192 [Still @ ~0.7 K]\n \u2191\n \u00b3He evaporates \u2192 pumped out\n`\n--\nTemperature Stages (Typical Path to mK)\n| Stage | Temperature | Method |\n|------|-------------|--------|\n| Room temp \u2192 77 K | Liquid nitrogen precooling |\n| 77 K \u2192 4 K | Liquid helium bath |\n| 4 K \u2192 1 K | \u2074He evaporation (1K pot) |\n| 1 K \u2192 ~50 mK | \u00b3He evaporation (still) |\n| Still \u2192 2\u201310 mK | \u00b3He\u2013\u2074He dilution |\n--\nWhy It Works So Well\nNo moving parts in the cold region \u2192 low vibration.\nContinuous operation (unlike \u00b3He\/\u2074He adsorption coolers).\nHeat of mixing provides cooling power proportional to \u00b3He flow rate:\n \\[\n \\dot\u007bQ\u007d \\approx 80 \\cdot \\dot\u007bn\u007d_3 \\cdot T^2 \\quad \\text\u007b(in nW, with \u007d \\dot\u007bn\u007d_3 \\text\u007b in \u00b5mol\/s, \u007d T \\text\u007b in mK)\u007d\n \\]\n--\nReal-World Performance\n| Parameter | Typical Value |\n|---------|---------------|\n| Base temperature | 2\u201310 mK |\n| Cooling power @ 100 mK | 100\u20131000 \u00b5W |\n| \u00b3He circulation rate | 10\u2013500 \u00b5mol\/s |\n| Hold time | Infinite (closed cycle) |\n--\nApplications\nQuantum computing: Cool superconducting qubits (Google, IBM, Rigetti).\nDark matter detectors (e.g., CDMS, EDELWEISS).\nNeutrino physics (CUORE, KATRIN).\nQuantum materials (heavy-fermion systems, topological matter).\nAstronomy (bolometers, MKIDs).\n--\nConnection to the Moon (Helium-3)\nEarth has very little \u00b3He (~few kg total, mostly from tritium decay).\nLunar regolith contains solar-wind-implanted \u00b3He (~10\u201320 ppb).\nA lunar dilution refrigerator could use locally mined \u00b3He to stay cold indefinitely in shadowed craters (~30 K ambient), enabling permanent quantum computers on the Moon.\n--\nSummary\n\u003e A dilution refrigerator cools by pumping \u00b3He across a phase boundary into a dilute \u2074He solution, absorbing heat via mixing entropy. It\u2019s the only practical way to reach continuous milli-Kelvin temperatures and is essential for frontier science and quantum A **dilution refrigerator** is a cryogenic device that achieves **continuous cooling to temperatures below 1 K** (as low as **~2–10 mK**, or 0.002–0.010 K) by exploiting the **quantum phase-separation** of liquid mixtures of the two helium isotopes: **³He** (helium-3) and **⁴He** (helium-4). It is the **standard tool** for ultra-low-temperature physics, quantum computing, astronomy detectors, and materials research requiring milli-Kelvin environments. --- ### Key Principle: ³He–⁴He Phase Separation 1. **Below ~0.87 K**, a mixture of ³He and ⁴He spontaneously separates into two immiscible liquid phases: - **Concentrated phase**: almost pure ³He (light, floats on top). - **Dilute phase**: mostly ⁴He with ~6.6% ³He dissolved in it (heavier, at the bottom). 2. **³He atoms prefer the concentrated phase**, but some dissolve into the dilute phase due to **entropy of mixing**. 3. **Fermi statistics** (³He is a fermion) make the dilute phase behave like a **Fermi liquid** with very low heat capacity. --- ### How Cooling Works (The Dilution Cycle) The core is a **mixing chamber** containing the two phases in contact. 1. **Evaporation-like process**: - ³He atoms **cross from the concentrated phase into the dilute phase**. - This **absorbs heat** because it increases entropy (mixing). 2. **³He is removed** from the dilute phase by pumping it away through a **still** (heated to ~0.7 K). - In the still, ³He evaporates more easily than ⁴He (due to zero-point energy differences). - Pumping lowers the ³He concentration in the dilute phase → more ³He flows in from the concentrated phase → **continuous cooling**. 3. **Circulation**: - Pumped ³He gas is cooled, condensed, and returned to the concentrated phase. - This forms a **closed refrigeration cycle** (no net helium loss). --- ### Schematic (Text-Based) ``` [External Pumps] ← ³He gas (warm) ↓ [Condenser] → liquid ³He → [Concentrated phase] ↓ ↑ ---------------------→ [Phase boundary] ↓ [Dilute phase] → [Still @ ~0.7 K] ↑ ³He evaporates → pumped out ``` --- ### Temperature Stages (Typical Path to mK) | Stage | Temperature | Method | |------|-------------|--------| | Room temp → 77 K | Liquid nitrogen precooling | | 77 K → 4 K | Liquid helium bath | | 4 K → 1 K | ⁴He evaporation (1K pot) | | 1 K → ~50 mK | ³He evaporation (still) | | Still → **2–10 mK** | **³He–⁴He dilution** | --- ### Why It Works So Well - **No moving parts** in the cold region → low vibration. - **Continuous operation** (unlike ³He/⁴He adsorption coolers). - **Heat of mixing** provides cooling power proportional to ³He flow rate: \[ \dot{Q} \approx 80 \cdot \dot{n}_3 \cdot T^2 \quad \text{(in nW, with } \dot{n}_3 \text{ in µmol/s, } T \text{ in mK)} \] --- ### Real-World Performance | Parameter | Typical Value | |---------|---------------| | Base temperature | 2–10 mK | | Cooling power @ 100 mK | 100–1000 µW | | ³He circulation rate | 10–500 µmol/s | | Hold time | Infinite (closed cycle) | --- ### Applications - **Quantum computing**: Cool superconducting qubits (Google, IBM, Rigetti). - **Dark matter detectors** (e.g., CDMS, EDELWEISS). - **Neutrino physics** (CUORE, KATRIN). - **Quantum materials** (heavy-fermion systems, topological matter). - **Astronomy** (bolometers, MKIDs). --- ### Connection to the Moon (Helium-3) - Earth has **very little ³He** (~few kg total, mostly from tritium decay). - **Lunar regolith** contains **solar-wind-implanted ³He** (~10–20 ppb). - A lunar dilution refrigerator could use **locally mined ³He** to stay cold **indefinitely** in shadowed craters (~30 K ambient), enabling **permanent quantum computers on the Moon**. --- ### Summary > A **dilution refrigerator** cools by **pumping ³He across a phase boundary** into a dilute ⁴He solution, absorbing heat via **mixing entropy**. It’s the only practical way to reach **continuous milli-Kelvin temperatures** and is essential for frontier science and quantum tech."}]}, "is_translatable": false, "legacy": {"bookmark_count": 50, "bookmarked": false, "conversation_id_str": "1985153293325418911", "created_at": "Mon Nov 03 04:06:51 +0000 2025", "display_text_range": [10, 41], "entities": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "timestamps": [], "urls": [{"display_url": "x.com/i/grok/share/D…", "expanded_url": "https://x.com/i/grok/share/DvQphMfhEo4QUD5s1hDJqIzn4", "indices": [18, 41], "url": "https://t.co/HfCEUsJDpN"}], "user_mentions": [{"id_str": "855305941", "indices": [0, 9], "name": "Andrew Côté", "screen_name": "Andercot"}]}, "favorite_count": 553, "favorited": false, "full_text": "@Andercot So cool https://t.co/HfCEUsJDpN", "id_str": "1985196951441412134", "in_reply_to_screen_name": "Andercot", "in_reply_to_status_id_str": "1985153293325418911", "in_reply_to_user_id_str": "855305941", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "possibly_sensitive": false, "possibly_sensitive_editable": true, "quote_count": 7, "reply_count": 76, "retweet_count": 44, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "44196397"}, "rest_id": "1985196951441412134", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "19452", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

models**—the new kid on the block that the 470-page monograph calls “the future of diffusion.” ### 1. The problem with classic diffusion Classic diffusion (like Stable Diffusion) works in **50–1000 tiny steps**: - Step 1: Add noise → blurry mess - Step 50: Denoise a little → vague shape - Step 1000: Sharp cat picture Each step is a small nudge. → Slow, expensive, and you can’t jump straight to the cat. ### 2. Enter the “flow-map” Imagine instead of 1000 baby steps, you learn **one single magic arrow** that points directly from pure noise to the perfect cat. That arrow is the **flow-map** Φ. ``` Noise ↦ (one shot) ↦ Cat x₀ Φ x₁ ``` Mathematically: ``` x₁ = Φ(x₀; θ) ``` - x₀ = random noise (same as diffusion start) - x₁ = final image - θ = neural-net parameters ### 3. How do you train Φ? You still use the same diffusion data (millions of denoising steps), but you **teach the network the whole journey in one leap**. Two clever tricks make it work: A. **Rectified Flow** - Turn the 1000-step zigzag path into a **straight highway**. - The monograph proves: a straight line is easier to learn in one step. B. **Flow Matching** - Instead of predicting noise, the network predicts **velocity** (“which direction and how fast”). - Loss = “how wrong is your arrow?” ### 4. Real-world super-powers - **1-step or 4-step generation** → 50× faster than Stable Diffusion - Same (or better) image quality - Works with guidance (text, depth maps, etc.) - Tiny models: a 300 M version beats 1000-step giants ### 5. One-picture summary ``` Classic diffusion: 🚶🚶🚶🚶🚶🚶🚶🚶🚶🚶 (50 steps) Flow-map: 🏎️💨 (1 step) ``` ### 6. Try it today - **Stable Diffusion 3.5** already ships a 4-step flow-map mode. - Open-source toys: - “Rectified Flow” demo (Hugging Face) - “Consistency Models” (same idea, earlier name) ### TL;DR Flow-map models = **diffusion on a rocket ship**. text:
You are a taker, not a maker. All you’ve done your whole life is take from the makers of the world. 

The zero-sum mindset you have is at the root of so much evil. Once you realize that civilization is not zero-sum and that it is about making far more than one consumes, then it becomes obvious that the path to prosperity for all is just let the makers make.

Regarding Tesla, the reality is that I have been given nothing. 

However, if I lead Tesla to become the most valuable company in the world by far and it stays that way for 5 years, shareholders voted to award me 12% of what is built. Anyone who wants to come along for the ride can buy Tesla stock.

If Tesla “merely” becomes a $1.999 trillion dollar company, I get nothing. This is a great deal for shareholders, which is why they voted so overwhelmingly to approve this, for which I am immensely grateful. 

And they did so by a margin far more than you won your political seat.

text:
The reason government programs are so inefficient is that, unlike a commercial company, the feedback loop for improvement is broken, because they have a state-mandated monopoly and can’t go out of business if customers are unhappy.

No matter how bad the service is at your DMV (sorry to pick on DMVs), you still have to use your DMV, because it’s a monopoly.

text:
Diffusion will obviously work on any bitstream.

With text, since humans read from first word to last, there is just the question of whether the delay to first sentence for diffusion is worth it.

That said, the vast majority of AI workload will be video understanding and generation, so good chance diffusion is the biggest winner overall.

Also means that the ratio of compute to memory bandwidth will increase.

This technology allows Megapack to resist changes on the network by creating its own voltage signal, preventing blackouts and allowing more renewables to come online https://t.co/bjpRCx5Rfn

text:
We should not take survival on Earth too much for granted.

No commercial opportunity, however large, is more important than ensuring the long-term survival of consciousness, which becoming multiplanetary enables.

Space habitats require mass transfer from elsewhere to grow. Planets do not. 

That said, a self-sustaining city on the Moon, while not as resilient to calamities that affect Earth as Mars (due to its proximity to Earth), is probably nonetheless worth the doing, in addition to Mars.

My experience voting in NYC:

1. No ID required 
2. Zohran on the ballot twice in the top row 
3. Eric Adams, who dropped out to avoid splitting the vote, still on the ballot 
4. Cuomo in the bottom right corner, 2nd row

• Ilya wrote a 52 page memo to get Sam fired and a separate doc on Greg https://t.co/ptMAmEHKN8
